titleEn: O Children of Weakness and Folly

verses.sadr: وبني البعد في الطباع على القرب في الصور
verses.ajuz: والشكول التي تباين في الطول والقصر

verses.sadr: أحتساء من الحرام وختما على الصرر
verses.ajuz: أين من كان قبلكم من ذوي البأس والخطر
سبقونا إلى الرحيل ، وإنا على الأثر

verses.sadr: قد نقلتم من القصور إلى ظلمة الحفر
verses.ajuz: حيث لا نضرب القباب عليكم ، ولا الحجر

translationEn: O you children of deficiency and moral lessons, children of weakness and frailty — you who are distant in nature though near in appearance. Where are those who came before you, the mighty and the daring? Ask the cities about them and seek out their news. They departed before us on that final journey, and we follow in their footsteps. Death strikes faster than the blink of an eye. You will be moved to tombs of earth, from palaces to the darkness of the grave. May God have mercy on the Muslim who remembered God and restrained himself, and may God forgive the one who feared and clothed himself in caution.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: lessons, moral warnings drawn from events
weakness, feebleness, lack of resolution
kinds, types, varying forms
bundles of money, purses tied with cord
the trace, the footstep left behind
clods of earth, clay (burial imagery)
restrained himself, was deterred
felt caution inwardly, internalized wariness

context: A rare ascetic poem (zuhd) from Abu Nuwas, admonishing contemporaries about the transience of life and the certainty of death. Despite his fame for wine poetry, Abu Nuwas also composed serious devotional and moral verse.

titleEn: Counsel to Oneself on Patience

verses.sadr: أكبر الأشياء عن أصغر عفو الله أصغر
verses.ajuz: ليس للإنسان ، إلا ما قضى الله وقدر

translationEn: The poet addresses himself by name — 'O Nuwas, show gravity and composure, and be patient.' Time has harmed you in some things, but what has pleased you is far greater. O you of great sin, God's forgiveness exceeds even your greatest transgression — for even the smallest of God's pardons surpasses the greatest of all worldly things. There is nothing for man except what God has decreed and ordained, and no creature has true governance of affairs — God alone is the Disposer of all things.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: show gravity and dignity, carry yourself with gravitas
show composure, act with beautiful conduct
time, fate, the course of the ages
pardon, forgiveness, clemency
decreed and ordained (divine predestination)
the Disposer, the One who manages and arranges all affairs

context: A self-admonishing poem in which Abu Nuwas addresses his own soul by name, urging patience and reliance on God's vast forgiveness. The theological emphasis on divine predestination and the immensity of God's pardon gives the poem a distinctly devotional character.

titleEn: The Seeker of God Shall Triumph

verses.sadr: فارغب إلى الله ، لا إلى بشر
وارغب إلى الله ، لا إلى جسد

translationEn: O seeker of God, you have attained victory and a wholesome, untainted gift. Turn your desire toward God, not toward a mortal who is ever-changing and decaying; not toward a body that passes from youth to old age. He whose petitioners are never disappointed is of a nature unlike that of mortal men — His essence is unlike human essence. Do you busy yourself with trivialities while the guarantee of safety from hellfire rests in your very hands?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: victory, success, triumph
gift, bounty, gracious bestowal
wholesome, pleasant, untroubled
turbidity, bitterness, trouble
decay, deterioration, wearing away
nonsense, idle trivialities, worthless occupations
hellfire (a Quranic name for hell)

context: A devotional poem contrasting the transience of human wealth and body with the permanence of divine generosity. The final verse — challenging the reader directly about hellfire — is a sharp homiletic turn characteristic of Abu Nuwas's zuhd poetry.

titleEn: Going Out with Keen Grey Falcons
translationEn: The poet sets out at dawn with keen, grey-blue falcons — pure-bred, with fine feathers and markings — trained in the open grounds of Nu'man, hunting for provisions and reward. They adorn the falconer's hand and glove through many a long and swift pursuit. One falcon, dubbed Abu Kazzaz, is bold and experienced, abundantly skilled, having been trained in fortified grounds and tuned by the open-field cricket's call. He cleaves through the middle of the skies with curved, accurate talons like the awls of a skillful cobbler, hunting alone without an escort. Indeed, the well-bred falcon trained by trainers of hawks is the finest companion in times of need.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grey-blue (describing the falcon's color)
sharp, keen, cutting (of hunting birds)
downy feathers, fine plumage
distinguishing mark, embroidery, fine pattern
the falconer's glove or gauntlet
abundant in strikes, prolific at hunting
need, want, a moment of poverty or difficulty

context: A poem in praise of the art of falconry (الصيد بالجوارح), which was a prestige pursuit in Abbasid court culture. Abu Nuwas deploys highly technical hunting vocabulary to describe the falcon's qualities, demonstrating his mastery of the genre beyond wine poetry.

titleEn: The Falcon Surpasses the Sparrowhawk
translationEn: The sparrowhawk is not without merit, but people unanimously prefer the falcon — the falcon hunts the great crane without hesitation, while the sparrowhawk's greatest effort amounts to no more than a chirping chick. This brief epigram uses the two hunting birds as a metaphor for comparing levels of excellence.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sparrowhawk, a small hawk
falcon, the prized hunting bird of the Abbasid courts
crane, a large and difficult bird to hunt
does not waver, does not turn back
a chirping or jumping young chick

context: A witty two-verse epigram (نتفة) contrasting two hunting birds to illustrate degrees of excellence. The compressed comparison is characteristic of Abu Nuwas's mastery of concise satirical and observational verse.

titleEn: The Abandoned Tavern of Wine Companions

verses.sadr: مساحب من جر الزقاق على الثرى ،
لم أدر من هم ؟ غير ما شهدت به

translationEn: The poet pauses before an abandoned gathering-place of wine companions who have departed at nightfall, leaving fresh and faded traces — drag marks from wine skins hauled across the soil, and scattered bundles of basil both fresh and dry. He held his own companions there, renewing their bonds, and he is inclined to linger at such places again. He dwelt there four days, wine circling in a golden Persian cup adorned with paintings inherited from Khosrow, its sides depicting white antelopes chased by mounted archers. The wine fills whatever opens in their collars, and water fills what turns in their caps.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wine companions, drinking friends (plural of نديم)
they departed at night, traveled through the darkness
drag marks, traces left by dragging
wine skins, leather wine flasks
basil, sweet-scented herb
a golden cup or vessel
paintings, pictorial designs
white antelopes, gazelles (a classical beauty metaphor)

context: A khamriyyah that opens with the classical elegiac motif of the abandoned campsite (أطلال), here transformed into a deserted wine companions' tavern. The Persian cup bearing Khosrow's painted imagery reflects the cosmopolitan Abbasid cultural setting.

titleEn: Time Gnawed Me and Love Struck Me Down

verses.sadr: أضلني بعدما كنت في العبادة قسا
verses.ajuz: لا أستفيق صلاة ، ولا أفتر درسا

verses.sadr: هلا طرقت ، ولم تلق في القراطق شمسا
verses.ajuz: فقلت : يا نور عيني ، خلست عقلي خلسا

verses.sadr: فما تمالك حتى افترى علي وخسا
verses.ajuz: فاسود وجهي منه ، حتى تحول نقسا

translationEn: Time has consumed the poet bit by bit, and love has struck him down further into weakness. His beloved's love has attached itself to his heart like a saddle-cloth to a beast, mixing with his soul until it became his very breath. Love led him astray after he had been devout — never missing a prayer, never ceasing to study. His reason flew away, leaving no trace; he blames his own glancing eye. He pleads to his beloved: 'Light of my eyes, you have stolen my mind — return my life with a bite of your mouth and a lick.' The beloved rejects him, lying about him and cursing; the poet's face darkens with shame as dark as ink. Time, he laments, brings him nothing but insults and diminishment, and no beloved he has known is harder-hearted or crueler.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: nibbling, gnawing bit by bit (as a predator gnaws)
relapse, collapse, being struck down again
a saddlecloth; metaphor for something always attached
a bishop or Christian monk; used for an intensely devout person
by theft, stealthily, surreptitiously
garments, tunics (collar area near the throat)
ink, pitch-black (turning as dark as ink with shame)
diminishment, undervaluing, treating someone as worthless

context: An intense ghazal in which Abu Nuwas charts the progression from devout ascetic life to complete mental dissolution through love. The ironic self-portrait as a fallen devout scholar (قس) is a recurring autobiographical motif in his poetry, playing on the tension between piety and passion.

titleEn: Despair of People's Loyalty

verses.sadr: كم صاحب قد كان لي وامقا
أقول لو قد نال هذا الغنى ،
حتى إذا صار إلى ما اشتهى ،

translationEn: The poet counsels himself and the listener to despair of people's loyalty — for true wealth lies in that very despair. He recalls a companion who was devoted to him when that companion was bankrupt, and the poet thought: if this man ever gains wealth, he will honor me above all. But once the man attained his desires and was counted among the prosperous, he severed every bond of friendship with heaps of gold coins, not even content with a mere axe-blow's worth of severance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: despair, hopelessness (positively: liberation from dependence on others)
wealth, richness, self-sufficiency
deeply loving, affectionately devoted
bankruptcy, poverty, having nothing
a great sum of gold, a large talent of money
the rope of friendship and purity
axe; here metaphor for a sharp, decisive break

context: A satirical poem about fair-weather friendship and the corrupting effect of wealth on loyalty. Abu Nuwas uses the personal experience of betrayal by a newly prosperous friend to make a universal observation about how prosperity changes people's relationships.

titleAr: ألا ليت شعري هكذا أنت للناس
titleEn: Are You Like This with Everyone?

verses.sadr: ألا ليت شعري هكذا أنت للناس ،
verses.ajuz: فأقدع عنك القلب يا صاح بالياس

verses.sadr: فقد كنت دهرا لا تروق لمعجب ،
verses.ajuz: سواي ، ولا تنمي إخائي إلى باس

verses.sadr: ولكنني لما بدا منك ما بدا ،
إذن ليس تزري بي لديك مودتي ،

translationEn: The poet cries out wishing he knew whether his beloved treated everyone the same way — for if so, he could restrain his heart through despair. For ages, the beloved seemed to please no admirer but him, and his fraternal affection seemed to lead nowhere troubling. But when the beloved's true nature revealed itself and he weighed his situation by a measuring scale, he understood: it is not his affection that diminishes him in the beloved's eyes, but rather his poverty that diminishes the beloved's love for him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: would that I knew, I wish I could know
to restrain, curb, hold back from
to please, delight, appeal to someone
brotherhood, fraternal friendship
a standard of measure, a scale or gauge
to belittle, diminish, bring shame upon

context: A compact ghazal blending romantic longing with bitter social observation, as Abu Nuwas realizes that his poverty — not his character — is the reason for the beloved's coldness toward him.

titleEn: Paper Merchants Who Cannot Spare a Sheet
translationEn: The poet requests a mere scrap of paper, yet his companions — most of whom are paper merchants by trade — cannot spare him one. May God curse their friendship and acquaintance, for their wealthy men are no different from their paupers when it comes to stinginess.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a piece of paper, a writing sheet
papers, sheets; also paper merchants or dealers
may God curse them (imprecation of disgrace)
the wealthy, those of comfortable means
the penniless, the utterly bankrupt

context: A sharp two-verse satirical epigram (نتفة) lamenting the stinginess of friends. The irony of paper merchants refusing to spare a scrap of paper is a biting piece of social comedy typical of Abu Nuwas's concise wit.

titleEn: The Good Men Are Gone, Leaving Wretches
translationEn: The true men have departed and risen high; we are left behind among the vilest of sub-human wretches. Every time the poet comes seeking benefit from them, they preempt his request with despair before he even asks. They weep before him until he wishes he could escape by bashing his head against theirs. You count them among the many, but when you examine them closely, they are not true men at all.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rose, departed, became fully independent and elevated
mythical sub-human creatures; used for vile or worthless beings
attainment, getting benefit or favor from someone
they rushed ahead of me, preempted me, got there first
with hopelessness, with despair as their answer
a multitude, a large enumerable number

context: A satirical poem (هجاء) lamenting the degeneracy of contemporary society, contrasting the noble men of the past with the contemptible ones who remain. The closing verse — 'they are not men at all' — delivers a devastating punch line.

titleEn: Describing a Keen Hunting Hound

verses.ajuz: من غير ما بيع ولا مكاس

verses.sadr: كم تيس رمل لاح في الكناس ،

translationEn: The poet describes a hunting dog trained on the drill — with the sunken temples of its skull exposed, rolling two eager, fiery eyes like glowing embers. Its eyes glow like the doe-eyed gazelle's, and its sleek body is like a branch of myrtle. What a fine companion and consoling brother it is — without price or bargaining. How many a desert mountain goat, glimpsed in the thicket, has it wrestled into the dust at the sides of the encampments. No one but Abu Nuwas has been given a dog like this.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I describe, I praise by detailed description
trained by beating on brass/copper (a training method)
the skull sutures, the bones of the head
the two eye sockets, the orbital hollows
the graceful, swaying gazelle fawn
myrtle tree, known for its slender branches
the one who consoles, who gives comfort in hardship

context: A poem of the hunting dog genre (وصف الكلب) within broader nature poetry. Abu Nuwas praises the hound's physical and hunting qualities with technical vocabulary, ending with a self-compliment that no one else was worthy of such a dog.

titleEn: How Are You, O Muhammad ibn Quraysh?

verses.ajuz: فيم ذا بل علام ذا أم لأيش ؟

verses.sadr: نحن في حان تاجر عندنا اللـه

translationEn: The poet greets his friend Muhammad ibn Quraysh, asking how his morning is, and playfully lamenting how his friend could justify abandoning him. They are in a merchant's tavern, sober in mind and measured in reason, not reckless. The wine brought from Tayzinabaadh is the pinnacle of all life's pleasures. He invites the friend to come drink with them at this early hour, expressing hope to one day see him commanding an army. He closes with a backhanded compliment: the friend's stinginess rivals the generosity of Ibn Hubaysh — a comic inversion.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may you never lose (a blessing formula)
my abandonment, throwing me aside, neglecting me
a tavern, a wine-house
recklessness, impulsivity, rashness
to drink the morning cup, to have a morning drink
generosity, open-handedness, magnanimity

context: A convivial poem (مجون) inviting a friend to join a wine session in a tavern, blending warm greeting, gentle reproach, and comic flattery. The reference to Tayzinabaadh (a district near Baghdad) grounds the poem in the Abbasid urban setting.

titleEn: A Languid Beloved Who Reproaches the Poet

verses.sadr: أقول له يوما ، وقد شفني الهوى :
verses.ajuz: أطلت عذابي فيك يا خير من نشا

verses.sadr: فقال : ألما يأن أن تترك الصبا
verses.ajuz: ومالك يا هذا ! وما لي ! وما تشا !

translationEn: A gazelle of a youth — languid and soft, effeminate in manner, the most beautiful of creatures and the most graceful of walkers. The poet tells him one day, his passion having worn him through: 'You have prolonged my torment, O finest of those who ever grew up.' The beloved replies sharply: 'Is it not yet time for you to abandon youthful folly? What is wrong with you? What do you want from me? Do whatever you wish!'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gazelle; used as a term of beauty for a beloved
languor, softness, a languid quality in the eyes
effeminacy, feminine softness of manner
wore me through, consumed me, weakened me entirely
passion, desire, love
youthful folly, the frivolity of youth

context: A brief ghazal in which the beloved's withering reply — scolding the poet for not outgrowing his passion — turns the romantic plea into an unexpectedly comic and sharp exchange. The three-verse form captures a complete dramatic encounter.

titleEn: True Praise for the Caliph al-Amin

verses.sadr: قد ينقص القمر المنير ، إذا استوى

translationEn: The poet offers praise to Caliph al-Amin Muhammad — praise given without any expectation of commercial return. His praise of al-Amin is truthful, while other panegyrics are mere fabrications and empty invention. The full moon wanes when it reaches its peak, but the radiance of Muhammad's face does not diminish. And when the Abbasids are counted bead by bead, Muhammad is their refined and extracted ruby.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: praise, laudation, commendation
the Trustworthy (title of Caliph Muhammad al-Amin)
waiting in anticipation, watching for an opportunity (here: seeking reward)
fabrication, making things up, baseless invention
radiance, splendor, magnificent beauty
the refined ruby, the purified gemstone

context: A panegyric addressed to the Abbasid Caliph Muhammad al-Amin (r. 809-813 CE), one of Abu Nuwas's key patrons. The poet distinguishes his sincere praise from the flattery of court poets who seek reward, positioning himself as offering genuine admiration.

titleEn: A Message of Harsh Reproach for Hamdan
translationEn: Tell Hamdan — though sincere counsel is not in my nature — that he is neither a free man to be blamed and corrected, nor a slave to be disciplined with the rod. May God's mercy be upon Adam — a mercy both universal and particular — for had Adam known that someone like Hamdan would descend among his children, he would have castrated himself to prevent his lineage.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my nature, my customary character
to be blamed, reproached, held accountable
I seek to appease or discipline him
encompassed all, was general and universal
particularized, showed special favor to individuals
castrated himself (a strong satirical hyperbole)

context: A devastating satirical poem (هجاء) addressed to a person named Hamdan. The closing verse — wishing Adam had castrated himself to prevent Hamdan's birth — is among the most extreme examples of Abu Nuwas's caustic satirical wit.

titleEn: Gazelles of the Royal Court

verses.sadr: إذا ما أوتر الموتر منهم عجل النبضا
verses.ajuz: وإن أقرض ذا هذا نوالا عجل النبضا

translationEn: In the royal chancery are gazelles — their eyes languid with a sickly beauty — bred in the palaces of eternity, knowing no sleeplessness, never accustomed to crouching in the open wastelands, never having left the comforts and ease of life since they came to be. They refer all matters to a glittering-eyed master who has already resolved them — an imam who is tyrannical, harsh, and whose word becomes the accepted ruling. When one of them is agitated, his pulse races; when one lends another a gift, the pulse races as well. Were it not that fish devour each other, O calamity, they would have filled the whole earth with their teeming numbers.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the royal chancery, administrative court
gazelles; used metaphorically for beautiful courtiers
those raised under protection, foster-daughters of palaces
eternity, paradise; here used for permanent palace residence
sleeplessness, the inability to close one's eyes
the open desolate wasteland, flat featureless desert
one with strikingly beautiful eyes, with intensely dark irises

context: A complex satirical ghazal targeting effete courtiers at the royal chancery, using the gazelle metaphor ironically. The poem blends erotic imagery, political satire, and a wry observation about the natural order — all fish eat each other — as a commentary on court survival.

titleEn: O You Who Turn Away, My Soul Is Your Ransom
translationEn: O you who turn away — my soul is your ransom, say that to one who turns away from you in return. Has my love for you, my master, dissolved so quickly? You hate me, my lord, yet I ransom you with a love that is itself hated. I remain as one fasting through your anger, waiting for your contentment to break my fast. How strange is the one who blames the lover — has he not himself both loved and hated? Does he see the path of these two emotions as one already traveled? Or was he simply indifferent, knowing neither, and thus finished? I have yearning while he has forgetfulness — while I stay awake, he sleeps.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who turns away, averts his face
my soul is your ransom (expression of extreme devotion)
dissolved, broken apart, unraveled
fasting through your anger (metaphor of patient endurance)
your contentment will break my fast (beautiful metaphor)
yearning, passionate longing, youthful love-sickness
forgetfulness, consolation, moving on from grief

context: A tender ghazal in which the poet addresses a beloved who has turned away, using the extended metaphor of fasting and breaking fast to describe the pain of estrangement and the hope of reconciliation.

titleEn: When You Were Beardless Your Beauty Shone
translationEn: Why did you not yield then, when your face was radiant in the freshness of youth, with barely a hair on your cheeks? Now that a beard has grown upon your face, it has taken away your charm — enough to fill a grasping hand. You are like fine wine that has been left: after all the sweetness, the pressed grape has turned into sharp vinegar.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the water of the face; its radiance and luster
the first budding of youth, the onset of adolescence
the cheek, the side of the face where facial hair first grows
charm, wit, attractiveness (literally 'salt')
the finest first-pressed wine
wine vinegar, sour vinegar made from wine

context: A brief ghazal lamenting the end of youthful beauty with the growth of the beard. The wine-turned-vinegar simile is a classic literary image that Abu Nuwas deploys with characteristic economy and precision.

titleEn: The Yolk Is Gone and Only the Membrane Remains
translationEn: The yolk has gone, and time has left nothing but the membrane and the empty shell. Goodness and kindness will not return until a chick hatches from under an elephant's egg — a thing that cannot be. Yet perhaps God will open up a basin of generosity and good deeds anew.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the egg yolk; metaphor for the best and richest part
the thin membrane inside an eggshell
the empty eggshell after the chick has hatched
goodness, kindness, generosity
hatches, emerges (from the egg)
a basin, a reservoir (of generosity)

context: A three-verse poem of resigned despair about the disappearance of generosity and goodness in the poet's world, expressed through a striking egg metaphor. The final verse pivots to a quiet hope in God's ability to restore what has been lost.

titleEn: O Beloved Whose Illness Worsened My Heart
translationEn: O beloved who is ill — your illness has increased the sickness of my heart, and this was done against my will, not by my consent. May the Most Merciful turn harm away from you and onto me; let my own soul be the captive that bears the worst of fate's decrees. What does relentless time want from me, curse it — I had barely stopped fearing time before it rose to intercept me again.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: against my will, in spite of me
turned away, deflected, averted
with my soul as a hostage or shackle
the worst of what fate decrees
curse it, woe to it (exclamation of distress)
stood in the way, intercepted, rose against

context: A brief but emotionally dense ghazal addressed to a sick beloved, where the poet offers himself as a substitute for the beloved's suffering. The final verse widens the lens to a lament about the cruelty of fate in general.

titleEn: O You Who Embody Pure Beauty
translationEn: O you who embody pure and unmixed beauty, who sway like a fresh and supple branch — if my life were to make you angry, I would kill myself just to earn your contentment.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gathered, contained, embodied in full
purely, unmixed, unadulterated
swayed, quivered, moved gracefully
fresh and supple, newly grown (of a branch)
made you angry, provoked your wrath
so that you might be pleased, to earn your contentment

context: A two-verse love epigram of extreme devotion. The hyperbolic willingness to die for the beloved's contentment is a conventional ghazal motif, expressed here with characteristic Abu Nuwas economy — all meaning compressed into two verses.

titleEn: Drink Without Restraint, God Forgives
translationEn: Abandon restraint in drinking, and take the cup with eagerness — drink from the chestnut-brown wine that flashes like a Persian lightning bolt, glowing inside the large drinking vessels. Do not hold back, for God's forgiveness will be freely dispensed tomorrow at the Bridge of the Straight Path. Forgiveness was created for none other than the sinning human being.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: falling short, holding back, restraining oneself
chestnut-brown, dark reddish-brown (of wine)
like the lightning of Persia (poetic expression for brilliant flash)
large drinking bowls, wide wine cups
the Bridge (on the Day of Judgment in Islamic eschatology)
a sinner, one who transgresses

context: A characteristic Abu Nuwas khamriyyah that audaciously uses Islamic eschatological concepts — God's forgiveness at the Sirat bridge — to justify drinking. This theological playfulness is one of his most controversial and distinctive rhetorical moves.

titleEn: Love Broke My Vitality
translationEn: Love has broken the poet's vitality — and he had been full of energy. A message reached him about the beloved that increased his despair. He cries out in loss: is someone like him expected to find a companion among mixed company? He resolves to associate only with the family of Amr or with Luqayt. He observes how many Arab women have maintained intimate relationships with Nabataean men. Had the beloved wanted union, the poet says, they would not have extracted such conditions of modesty.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: vitality, energy, enthusiasm
despair, hopelessness, despondency
a mixed companion, one of mixed origin
the Nabataeans, non-Arab rural inhabitants of Iraq
union, connection, intimacy with the beloved
shyness, bashfulness, modest reserve

context: A ghazal of frustrated love that takes an unexpected turn into social commentary on inter-ethnic relationships in Abbasid Iraq, using the Nabataeans as a foil to argue that tribal and ethnic pride is no obstacle to love when the desire is genuine.

titleEn: A Beautiful Youth by the Nile
translationEn: A youth of wondrous creation, full in his features, slender at the waist like a tethered mare — his father is from the great Copts of Egypt, a lineage far above the Nabataeans. He gave the poet the clear water of the Nile to drink at ease, along with an expansive wine from the vineyards of the villages of Asyut — wine with two qualities of taste and scent, and a color in the glass like sesame oil. They were alone together: the poet contending with him over the wine cup and reciting to him poems in the Basit meter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: of wondrous creation, marvelously formed
full in his facial lines and features
tethered, a tethered horse (implying slender, controlled grace)
Copts, the indigenous Christian inhabitants of Egypt
a wide, expansive wine; also pure wine
sesame oil (used to describe the wine's golden color)
wine (specifically wine drunk communally, a type)

context: A khamriyyah set in Egypt, combining the poet's praise of a Coptic youth with a description of Egyptian wine from the vineyards of Asyut. The poem reflects Abu Nuwas's travels and his characteristic interweaving of erotic admiration with wine description.

titleEn: I Traded Vitality for Brokenness Through Love
translationEn: The poet has exchanged vitality for brokenness, and love has bound him with affliction's ropes. Were it not that he wields patience over his heart, it would burst from its sinews. A fool told him: 'If only you would stop!' The poet replied: 'Our meeting will be on the Bridge of Judgment.' Had the fool not made himself a guest on his carpet when he rebuked him, the poet would have shown him reason enough to excuse those who fall for the passion of full-figured houris. Your blame of me, and your saying 'leave him' — these fall harder on me than the lash of whips.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: brokenness, a fracturing of spirit and vitality
the sinews attaching the heart, the pericardium
a fool, a stupid person (strong insult)
the Bridge over Hell (on the Day of Judgment)
the full-figured houris, the women of paradise with full shapes
whips, lashes (plural of سوط)

context: A ghazal of stubborn passion, where Abu Nuwas dismisses the reproacher's counsel with characteristic defiance, using an eschatological metaphor — 'we'll meet on the Bridge of Judgment' — to underscore his unyielding attachment to his beloved.

titleEn: The Hunting Dog Tears Through Fresh Prey
translationEn: The hunting dog tears through the prey when the quarry is fresh — with claws dark as the soot of cooking stones smeared with grease. It pricks up its ears in alertness, so that the two hollows of its skull resemble parallel slits. The hounds rush like something snatched up instantly, faster than a sandgrouse can cry its name. They prey upon the spotted desert gazelles, meeting from this dog an inescapably harsh judge — shattering bones and tearing fresh hides like craftsmen cutting fine cloth, when blood mingled with dust turns grey-white. Praise be to God for what He has bestowed!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: tears apart, rips through
fresh, recently slaughtered; untouched prey
with its claws (plural of برثن)
the three cooking stones of a hearth
smeared, covered in grease or soot
the sandgrouse's cry of its own name (قطا); a proverbial expression of speed)
blood, especially fresh blood of game

context: A hunting poem (طرد) describing a dog's ferocious hunting prowess in vivid, technical detail. Abu Nuwas closes with a pious praise of God for the gift of such a hunting companion, a conventional closing move that frames the secular hunt within a devotional frame.

titleEn: A Fierce Dog Prepared for the Hunt
translationEn: The poet has prepared a fierce dog for the chase — when it sets out in the morning from hunger it blazes with intensity. It pulls at the leash and insists stubbornly, as though a devil has been set upon it to urge it on. It presses flock after flock of gazelles relentlessly until you see them shattering apart in terror. Every day it wins its share of the hunt until you see the blood of the prey pooling and spreading.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the pursuit hunt, chasing game on horseback or by dogs
fierce, harsh, rough in temperament
gluttony, intense hunger, greed for food
blazed, burned, flamed (with intensity or hunger)
persisted stubbornly, clung tenaciously
presses hard upon, pursues relentlessly
spread out, pooled, pressed onto the ground

context: A compact six-verse hunting poem (طرد) describing a hunting dog's ferocious energy from the moment it is unleashed through its successful daily hunt. The demonic simile — 'as though a devil has been set upon it' — vividly captures the dog's uncontrollable drive.

titleAr: ما مثل هذا اليوم في طيبه
titleEn: No Day Finer Than This for Wine

verses.sadr: ما مثل هذا اليوم في طيبه

verses.ajuz: تحب في ذا اليوم أن نصنعا ؟ !

verses.sadr: هل لك أن تغدو على قهوة

translationEn: No day is as fine as this in its sweetness — it has not been wasted without amusement nor spent idly. What do you think of it? What would you like us to do on this day? How about starting the morning with a cup of coffee-like wine that courses quickly through a man when he downs it quickly? People have never found, nor have they ever experienced, anything that drives away sorrow as effectively as it does.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its goodness, its pleasantness, its fine quality
spent without use, left idle, wasted
wine (an early meaning of قهوة before it came to mean coffee)
moves quickly through, acts swiftly within
grief, worry, sorrow
that which drives away, a repellent of sorrow

context: A convivial invitation poem that captures a perfect day and invites a companion to spend it drinking. Notable for its use of قهوة in its original sense of wine — long before the word came to denote coffee in Arabic.

titleEn: I Have Abandoned Folly on the Caliph's Command

verses.ajuz: هي اليوم حرب ، وهي أمس شياع

translationEn: O reproacher, I have sold off my foolishness in the market where it is traded, and bared my head with nothing left to veil it. The Commander of the Faithful has forbidden me from youthful abandon, and the Commander of the Faithful's command is to be obeyed. A pleasure I have abandoned out of the Imam's reproof — though it held for the pleasure-seeker both spectacle and sound. He who is saturated with the water of youth seems to thirst and hunger from the tightening of his belly. I have restricted my soul from wine — which today is forbidden, though yesterday it was freely shared.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the reproacher, the one who blames and rebukes
youthful folly, the frivolity of youth
one whose command is obeyed, a followed authority
the rebuke of the Imam/Caliph
the tightening and thinning of the belly (from abstinence)
wine, especially wine drunk continuously
freely shared, spread widely, open to all

context: A poem of ostensible repentance and compliance with the Caliph's prohibition on the poet's wine-drinking and youthful behavior. The ambiguous, almost ironic tone — describing the pleasures he 'gave up' in loving detail — leaves open whether Abu Nuwas is sincere or merely performing compliance.
